paper_title: Moving to the Adjacent Possible: Discovering Paths for Export Diversification in Rwanda
research_answer: Rwanda's trade collapse in the 1980s was dramatic and illustrates the fundamental vulnerability of economies dependent on a handful of commodities. Exports as a share of GDP fell from a high of 21% in 1979 to just 6% in 1990 — a drop of 15 percentage points over a single decade. This decline was almost certainly driven by external commodity price shocks, since Rwanda's export revenue was concentrated in coffee, tea, and tin, leaving the economy with virtually no buffer when prices for these goods fell on world markets. The fact that Rwanda has less than $50 per capita in exports even decades later suggests the economy never fully recovered its export capacity from that period.
supporting_quotes: As a result, exports of goods and services fell precariously from a high of 21 percent of GDP in 1979 to a low of 6 percent of GDP in 1990
Three basic commodities – coffee, tea, and tin – made up more than 80 percent of the country's exports through its history and still drive the bulk of export growth today.
at less than US$50 per capita, Rwanda still has one of the lowest levels of exports in the world
